name: master tomato dal
instructions:
FOUR VARIANTS. All share the base masala engine (bloom whole spices → aromatic base → ground spices → tomato → legume + cooking liquid → simmer → finish with kasoori methi and cilantro), but the four variants differ meaningfully on onion treatment, tomato form, spice emphasis, and finish.

=== VARIANT A — CHOLE (MAXIMALLY AROMATIC, CHICKPEA, BLACK TEA + CARDAMOM + PUREED ONIONS) ===

The darkest, thickest, most aromatic variant. Follows the reddit reference recipe closely (see bottom of notes). Chole is the chickpea preparation that leans darker and heavier than chana masala — anardana / tea / cardamom / cloves / cinnamon are all doing structural work.

DISTINCTIVES:
- Chickpeas as the legume.
- Onions: BOILED (3-4 min in water) then BLENDED into a smooth puree. Non-negotiable — pureeing raw onions is bitter and aggressive.
- Whole spices bloomed in ghee/oil: bay leaf, green cardamom pods, cloves, cinnamon stick.
- Black tea in the bean-cooking liquid — this is the chole color/tannin signature. Cook the chickpeas in water with a tea bag + bay + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon, then reserve the spiced stock for the gravy.
- Asafetida in the ground spice mix (a pinch).
- Fresh tomatoes only, pureed. NO tomato paste.
- Ghee (or ghee + oil combination — the reddit recipe favors this).
- Cilantro finish, kasoori methi non-optional.
- Mint welcome (with cloves alongside) when the tea is doing its signature work — see mint-and-cloves note below.

=== VARIANT B — CHANA MASALA (CHICKPEA, LEMON + CILANTRO + MINT + PEPPERS, LIGHTER AND TANGIER) ===

The lighter chickpea preparation. Amchur- and lemon-forward, more tomato-visible, minimal or no black tea. Peppers are welcome. Onion form is flexible.

DISTINCTIVES:
- Chickpeas as the legume.
- Onions: FINELY DICED or PUREED preferred, but this variant TOLERATES chunky whole onions (unlike variant A's strict puree rule). Chunky onions are visible in the finished dish and read more rustic/homestyle.
- Whole spices in the bloom: bay leaf, green cardamom (in moderation).
- NO black tea (or minimal — this variant is not chole-shaped).
- Fresh tomatoes (chopped or lightly pureed) — more visible tomato presence than chole.
- Ghee or vegetable oil both fine.
- Amchur powder is the tang slot — heavier than in the other variants.
- Lemon AND lemon zest at the finish (zest > juice — brighter, less watery).
- Peppers: variety welcome — anaheim, serrano, jalapeño, even bell pepper.
- Cilantro and mint both at the finish. Kasoori methi too but subordinate to cilantro.
- NO cinnamon, NO cloves — those are chole territory.

=== VARIANT C — CHUNK LENTIL DAL (LENTIL, LOTS OF CHUNKY EVERYTHING, PRACTICAL/HOMESTYLE) ===

The most 'curry' of the four variants — chunky peppers, chunky onions of all kinds, chunky vegetables generally. Lentils go in dry alongside the masala; no separate pre-cook required (major convenience advantage over chickpea and rajma variants). Reads more like a chunky curry than a smooth stew.

DISTINCTIVES:
- Lentils as the legume. Mediterranean brown lentils are preferred here — they hold structure through the cook. Masoor dal (red lentils) technically works but they disintegrate more, and I'd rather use red lentils in coconut-based dishes (see coconut mung bean curry family) than in indian tomato dal. Save red lentils for the coconut path.
- Onions: CHUNKY of any color — red, yellow, or white all fine. Coarsely chopped, generously. This is the variant where you actually SEE onion pieces in the finished dish and that's the point.
- Peppers: LOTS of chunky peppers — anaheim, serrano, jalapeño, even bell pepper. This is the variant that most resembles a homestyle indian curry with visible chunky vegetables, so lean into the peppers and don't reach for the minimal single-pepper approach of the earlier framing.
- Whole spices bloomed: bay leaf, cardamom.
- Tomatoes: fresh, chopped coarse. Tolerates some tomato paste alongside for depth.
- Ghee or vegetable oil both fine.
- Finish: BOTH cilantro and kasoori methi.
- Practical advantage: lentils can go into the pot from dry alongside the masala. Soaking overnight helps but isn't strictly required. This is the reason this is the everyday variant — you get a large quantity of legume in the final dish without the pre-cook overhead of chole or rajma.

=== VARIANT D — RAJMA GRAVY (KIDNEY BEAN, GRAVY-CONSISTENCY, TOMATO PASTE + GHEE + CHILI POWDER FORWARD) ===

The gravy end of the family. Smooth and concentrated; the rajma (kidney beans) are structural, and everything else supports them. Follows Mexican-bean logic — minimize anything that distracts from the glutamates and the beans themselves.

DISTINCTIVES:
- Red beans (kidney beans) as the legume. Pre-cook or use canned.
- Onions: BOILED then BLENDED into a smooth puree (same as chole).
- Whole spices bloomed: bay leaf, cardamom.
- Tomatoes: TOMATO PASTE, as concentrated as possible. NO fresh tomatoes, no canned. This is the concentration that makes rajma read as gravy rather than stew.
- GHEE is NON-OPTIONAL. The gravy needs ghee's richness — vegetable oil alone reads thin.
- Kashmiri chili powder plays a bigger role here than in chole/chana masala. The color and gentle heat carry the dish.
- Kasoori methi is non-optional — the dried fenugreek is a load-bearing flavor.
- Cilantro is welcome but methi leads.
- NO amchur, NO lemon (rajma doesn't want the tang slot filled — kasoori methi + tomato paste + kashmiri chili is the flavor structure).
- NO mint, NO cloves (chole variant territory).
notes:
A FAMILY OF FOUR — SHARED SPICE BASE, DIVERGENT CHARACTERS: all four variants share the ground spice base (cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, kashmiri chili powder, cardamom, kasoori methi at finish). The variants diverge on onion treatment, tomato form, peppers, fat, additional whole spices, and finish.

CONSISTENCY AXIS: rajma (D) is the smooth-gravy end; chunk lentil dal (C) is the chunky-goo end; chole (A) is a thick aromatic stew; chana masala (B) is the middle tanginess.

=== ONION / TOMATO TEXTURE — TEXTURE LOGIC ACROSS THE FAMILY ===

The rule on whether to blend or chunk the onions and tomatoes comes down to what the legume itself contributes texturally to the final dish.

- CHOLE AND RAJMA (A AND D) — BLENDED/SMOOTH: in both, the BEANS themselves carry the main texture of the dish. Coarse onions and tomatoes compete with the beans for textural attention — you end up with a chunky-everything dish where nothing leads. Smooth onion-and-tomato bases stay out of the bean's way and let the bean be the structural element.
- CHANA MASALA (B) — FLEXIBLE, LEANS PUREED: chickpeas here are still the structural texture, so pureed onions are still preferred. But this variant is tangier and more visible-tomato-forward, and chunky onions don't ruin it the way they would in chole. Rustic/homestyle variant B is fine.
- LENTIL DAL (C) — CHUNKY: lentils break down during the cook into a kind of loose goo and lose their structural texture. Without coarse onions and tomatoes to add chew, each spoonful is a uniform paste. Chunky onion and chopped tomato restore textural variety and give the dish bite.

CHOLE HAS A SECOND REASON FOR BLENDING: in variant A the unflavored black tea is a signature ingredient, and it brings a fruity/floral character that needs balancing. Blended boiled onions (and a pinch of asafetida) give the masala a denser, sweeter aromatic base that absorbs the floral edge and pulls the dish into a cohesive read. Chunky onions in a chole variant leave the tea + cardamom floating on top.

BOIL BEFORE BLENDING (VARIANTS A AND D): pre-boil the onions in water for 3-4 min before pureeing — pureeing raw onion is bitter and aggressive. Non-negotiable for chole (A) and rajma (D).

SO THE RULE IS:
- Chole (A): boiled + blended onion; fresh tomatoes pureed; NO tomato paste.
- Chana masala (B): finely diced or pureed onion (chunky tolerated); fresh tomatoes chopped or lightly pureed.
- Lentil dal (C): chunky onion; chunky/chopped tomato; tolerates some tomato paste.
- Rajma (D): boiled + blended onion; tomato paste, concentrated.

=== CHOLE VS CHANA MASALA — WHY VARIANTS A AND B ARE BOTH CHICKPEA-BASED ===

Chole (variant A) and chana masala (variant B) are both chickpea dishes but represent genuinely different preparations, not just naming variants:
- CHOLE (A) leans DARKER, THICKER, more whole-spice-and-tea forward — black tea, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, sometimes anardana for tartness. The reddit reference recipe is chole.
- CHANA MASALA (B) leans LIGHTER, TANGIER, and amchur/lemon-forward. Skips the tea. More visible tomato. Peppers welcome. Mint at the finish.
In indian home cooking these are often blurred, but the axes above are the real distinction. Pick one direction; don't try to hedge.

CHOLE AND CHANA ALSO REFER TO DIFFERENT CHICKPEA VARIETIES: kabuli chana (large beige, what western supermarkets stock) vs kala chana (small dark brown/black, more common in indian grocery stores). Each has its own preferred dish style. Since I default to whatever the regular supermarket has (basically always kabuli chana), that legume-level distinction isn't captured in these recipes. Worth knowing it exists; not blocking on it if your pantry is the standard one.

=== VARIANT A NOTES (CHOLE) ===

MINT AND CLOVES IN CHOLE (BLACK TEA PATH): mint and cloves are both welcome additions in chole when the black tea is doing its signature work. This is a real application of the mint-and-tomato nuance from the herbs and greens article: mint clashes with tomato in simple aromatic dishes, but works in heavily-spiced ones. The chole spice base (cumin, coriander, garam masala, cardamom, kashmiri chili powder, amchur, methi, cloves, cinnamon) is exactly the heavy-spice-cabinet context the article describes — enough structural spice work to absorb mint's edge, and the tea+cardamom floral wash actually pairs with mint rather than fighting it. Cloves bridge the tea, cardamom, and mint into a cohesive whole. Both go in toward the end so they don't get cooked into oblivion.

CHOLE FAILURE MODES:

- TURMERIC IN THE TEA BROTH AS WELL AS IN THE PAN MAKES THE DISH TOO SPICY: turmeric belongs in the gravy/pan spice mix, not in the bean-cooking tea broth. Doubling it up (broth AND pan) compounds the heat and bitterness, especially when you've got jalapeños or other green chiles in the dish.

- AMCHUR + TEA BROTH + CARDAMOM TOGETHER GO OVERLY FLORAL: each of these three is a floral-leaning ingredient on its own. Combining all three pushes the dish past what the blended boiled onions and asafetida can absorb. The floral wash starts to dominate and the chole signature reads perfumed rather than savory. If using black tea in the broth and cardamom in the spice base, dial back the amchur (or skip it) and reach for lemon zest instead for the brightness slot.

=== VARIANT B NOTES (CHANA MASALA) ===

AMCHUR AND LEMON ARE THE TANG STACK: chana masala's brightness comes from amchur (dried mango powder) plus lemon (juice and zest). Zest > juice — the zest concentrates the citrus oils without adding water. Amchur is the earthy-sweet-tart complement to the citrus.

PEPPERS ARE WELCOME — VARIETY IS FINE: unlike the other variants, chana masala takes a whole lemon-and-peppers parade. Anaheim, serrano, jalapeño, even bell pepper if you want more vegetal presence. Green chiles specifically brighten this variant the way they don't in the darker chole.

MINT AT THE FINISH: mint stirred in raw at the end (with the cilantro) works well in chana masala for the same heavy-spice-cabinet reason it works in chole — the spice stack absorbs mint's edge. Don't cook it in.

NO CINNAMON OR CLOVES: those are chole territory (variant A). Adding them here pushes chana masala toward chole and loses the lighter tangier identity. If you find yourself reaching for cinnamon and cloves, make variant A instead.

ROSEMARY — WANT TO TRY: rosemary is untested here but I want to try it. The logic: variant B's lemon-forward, tomato-visible, peppers-and-mint character is closer to a mediterranean-adjacent indian register than the darker chole. Rosemary's piney depth might pair well with the amchur-and-lemon axis and add a second-dimension herb note the way it does in mediterranean tomato dishes. Untested; make a batch and see.

=== VARIANT C NOTES (LENTIL DAL) ===

PRACTICAL ADVANTAGE — DRY LENTILS INTO THE POT: this is the everyday variant because the lentils don't need a separate pre-cook. Soaking overnight helps (softer texture, faster cook) but isn't strictly required. You save the pressure-cook step that chole and rajma require. This is why lentil dal ends up in weeknight rotation while chole/rajma are weekend projects.

CHUNK IS THE POINT — DON'T PUREE THE ONIONS OR TOMATOES: the whole texture logic of variant C rests on coarse onions and chopped tomatoes providing bite against the disintegrating lentils. If you puree the base, the entire dish reads as uniform paste with no textural interest. This is the variant where chunky is a feature, not a compromise.

LOTS OF PEPPERS AND CHUNKY ONIONS OF ALL KINDS — VARIANT C IS THE MOST 'CURRY' OF THE FAMILY: variant C's flavor and texture read closer to a homestyle indian curry with visible chunky vegetables than to the smoother stew-like registers of the other three. Lean INTO the peppers — anaheim, serrano, jalapeño, and even bell pepper all belong, and generously. Chunky onions of all colors (red, yellow, white) are fine and expected. This is the variant where you actually see and taste discrete pepper and onion pieces in every spoonful, and that visibility is the point.

LENTIL CHOICE — MEDITERRANEAN BROWN LENTILS PREFERRED, RED LENTILS BELONG IN COCONUT DISHES: mediterranean brown lentils (or french green lentils, similar structural behavior) are what variant C is tuned for. They hold their shape through the cook and provide the structural bite that keeps the dish from turning into total paste. Masoor dal (red/orange lentils) works technically but disintegrates faster, and — more importantly — red lentils are better used in coconut-based dishes (see coconut mung bean curry family) where the coconut milk carries their softer texture. Save red lentils for the coconut path; use mediterranean brown lentils here.

=== VARIANT D NOTES (RAJMA) ===

GHEE IS NON-OPTIONAL: rajma is the ghee-forward variant. Vegetable oil alone reads thin against the concentrated tomato paste and kidney bean gravy. Use ghee generously, or ghee + a splash of oil.

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER LEADS: chili powder plays a bigger role in rajma than in the other variants. Bright red color, gentle heat, and it stands up to the tomato paste concentration.

KASOORI METHI IS NON-OPTIONAL AND CANONICAL FINISH: dried fenugreek leaves crumbled in at the end. Rajma leans on methi where the other variants use it as a supporting ingredient.

NO AMCHUR, NO LEMON, NO MINT: rajma doesn't want the tang slot filled — the gravy structure is tomato paste + methi + kashmiri chili + ghee. Adding amchur or lemon fights the concentrated register. Mint and cloves are chole territory.

=== SHARED / CROSS-VARIANT RULES ===

KASHMIRI CHILI POWDER IN ALL FOUR VARIANTS: color and gentle heat without overpowering. Different from generic chili powder. Non-optional across the family.

KASOORI METHI IN ALL FOUR VARIANTS: non-optional in rajma, welcome in the other three. Load-bearing flavor, not a garnish.

INDIAN-MEXICAN ALIGNMENT: the blended-base variants (A and D) bring these indian bean dishes structurally close to mexican bean cooking, which also relies on blended aromatic bases (sofritos, blended chile-onion-garlic pastes). Worth keeping in mind — bean dishes of both cuisines share more DNA than the spice profiles suggest.

=== REFERENCE RECIPE — REDDITOR CHANA MASALA (READS AS CHOLE — INFORMS VARIANT A) ===

A chana masala recipe found on reddit. This is actually chole-shaped (tea + cardamom + cloves + cinnamon in the bean broth), and it's the reference recipe for VARIANT A specifically. Captured here in full as a study reference.

FOR COOKING THE BEANS:
- 1 cup dried garbanzo beans (or 2 cans)
- 1 tsp tea or 1 tea bag
- 2 black cardamom pods, cracked open slightly
- 3 cloves
- ~1 inch cinnamon
- 2 bay leaves
- 1/8 tsp baking soda (skip if using canned)
- salt

FOR THE GRAVY:
- 3-4 tbsp oil/ghee (a combination is best)
- 2 medium onions (red/white/yellow all fine)
- 3 tomatoes
- 6 garlic cloves
- 1 inch ginger
- 2 green chilies

DRY SPICE MIX (mix all in a bowl, set aside) — author uses BOTH a custom mix AND a packaged chana masala blend; he insists this redundancy works better:
- 1 tsp red chili powder
- 1/2 tsp turmeric
- 1 tsp cumin powder
- 1 tsp coriander powder
- 1 tsp garam masala
- 1 tsp anardana (pomegranate seeds)
- 1/2 tsp rock salt
- pinch asafoetida
- 1 tsp kasoori methi
- 1 tsp amchur powder
- 3 tsp Everest chhole/chana masala blend (author's specific brand recommendation after testing 5 brands)

TEMPERING:
- 2 tbsp ghee
- 3 garlic cloves, finely chopped
- 1 tsp kashmiri red chili powder
- 1 tsp cumin
- 10-12 thinly sliced ginger juliennes

PREP:
- Boil the onions in water for 3-4 min BEFORE pureeing — pureeing raw onions gives a bitter taste; boiling first fixes this.
- Puree the tomatoes (blanching and skinning optional).
- Grind ginger and garlic into a paste with water; chop green chilies separately (or include in the paste).

COOK THE BEANS:
1. Soak the beans overnight.
2. In a pressure cooker, add 3-4 cups water + baking soda.
3. Tie tea, whole cardamom, cloves, and cinnamon in a small cloth bundle; add bay leaves separately.
4. Pressure cook beans for 3-4 whistles until soft. (Canned beans: skip baking soda, 1 whistle just to infuse.)
5. SEPARATE BEANS FROM STOCK — DO NOT THROW THE STOCK AWAY. The stock is essential.

GRAVY:
1. Heat oil/ghee in a pan.
2. Add pureed boiled onions, cook on medium / medium-high heat for 15-20 min (or more) until light brown, stirring occasionally.
3. Add ginger-garlic paste and chilies; cook a couple of minutes.
4. Add pureed tomatoes; cook on medium / medium-high for 15 min, stirring occasionally.
5. Add the ground dry spice mix; cook 3-4 min.
6. Add the beans, mix, cook a couple of minutes (subtle flavor develops here before stock goes in).
7. Add the reserved stock + salt; simmer 5-7 min.

TEMPERING (OPTIONAL — author says it elevates the dish):
1. Heat ghee in a small frying/tempering pan.
2. Add chopped garlic, cumin, ginger juliennes; fry briefly (don't brown).
3. Stir in kashmiri red chili powder.
4. Pour the tempering mixture carefully over the prepared chana masala.

FINISH: sprinkle cilantro on top.

THINGS WORTH LEARNING FROM THIS RECIPE:
- The bean-infusion step (cooking the beans in tea + whole spices + baking soda, then reserving the spiced stock for the gravy).
- The boil-the-onions-before-pureeing technique to avoid raw-onion bitterness.
- The double-spice approach (custom mix + packaged Everest chana masala blend) — possibly worth testing whether the redundancy is real.
- The closing tempering step (kashmiri chili in ghee with garlic, cumin, ginger juliennes, poured over at the end).
- 15-20 min onion cook AND 15 min tomato cook — substantially longer than most chana masala recipes call for.
